meta.decision_rationale: Explicit refusals clarify what the library will NOT do, even under pressure. These refusals protect core identity and prevent drift.
evidence_refs: Case studies of libraries that compromised values
tradeoffs: But lack of refusals risks slow value erosion
rejected_alternatives: No formal refusals (risk of incremental compromise)

refusals.refusal: We refuse to remove books based on political, religious, or cultural pressure—from any source.
refusals.boundary: We DO respond to legitimate content errors (factual corrections, accessibility); we DO NOT remove books for ideology.
refusals.escalation: If pressured to censor: (1) document request, (2) convene ethics committee, (3) explain decision publicly, (4) appeal to library association for support

refusals.refusal: We refuse to use corporate algorithms (Google, Amazon, Apple) to curate or recommend library collection.
refusals.boundary: We DO use open-source, community-controlled tools; we DO preserve human librarian judgment; we DO maintain community override.
refusals.escalation: If tempted by corporate partnerships: (1) audit for value alignment, (2) require community vote, (3) keep human librarians in decision loop

refusals.refusal: We refuse to track, profile, or monetize patron reading habits.
refusals.boundary: We DO collect aggregate usage stats for planning; we DO NOT create individual patron profiles or sell data.
refusals.escalation: If facing budget pressure to monetize data: (1) explore alternative funding, (2) convene board, (3) prefer to reduce hours rather than compromise trust

refusals.refusal: We refuse to create tiered access (premium collections for paid members, restricted collections for low-income patrons).
refusals.boundary: We DO have specialized collections for experts; they are free to ALL patrons. We DO NOT segregate by ability to pay.
refusals.escalation: If facing budget cuts that might force tiering: (1) convene board and community, (2) reduce scope before reducing equity, (3) seek alternative funding
